profile_bio.description: Observing Hyperliquid Ecosystem | $HYPE | check my tg calls: https://t.co/BvKDxHKL3E

kept.text:
Polymarket spent two years building the prediction market category. 

Kalshi spent two years trying to regulate it. Then they both handed Hyperliquid a blueprint to destroy them.

Here's what actually happened.

Hyperliquid published HIP-4 a proposal to add prediction markets directly into its platform. Binary contracts on real-world outcomes. 

Elections, macro data, crypto prices. Settled onchain. No switching platforms, no moving funds, everything inside the same margin account you already use for perps.

That's already a credible threat. But here's the part that makes it genuinely dangerous.

➝ The proposal was co-authored by John Wang, head of crypto at Kalshi.

Kalshi didn't just fail to block a competitor from entering prediction markets. 

It actively wrote the competitor's product spec, formalized a partnership with them in March, and handed them the regulatory credibility Hyperliquid couldn't build on its own.

That leaves Polymarket as the most exposed player in the space. And Polymarket had nothing to do with any of this.

➝ The numbers that explain why this matters:

On-chain researcher Fleck found that 3.3% of Polymarket users are already active on Hyperliquid. That group generates 12% of Polymarket's total volume. 

These are the whales. The highest-frequency, highest-volume traders already have a foot in both worlds.

HIP-4 gives them a clear reason to stop splitting their activity. One margin account. One platform. Everything in one place.

Losing 3.3% of your users sounds irrelevant. Until you realize that 3.3% is responsible for more than a tenth of your entire platform's revenue.

➝ The fee structure alone should concern Polymarket:

Hyperliquid's HIP-4 opens positions at zero cost. Fees only apply on close or settlement. 

Polymarket currently charges up to 2% on winning bets. For high-frequency traders that 2% compounds into a significant annual drag. The math isn't close.

➝ Now look at the capital context:

Kalshi raised $1B at a $22B valuation in March. Polymarket is raising $400M at a $15B valuation right now. 

These are not small startups. This is an arms race between two heavily capitalized incumbents and Hyperliquid just walked in with $219B in monthly trading volume already on its platform and a zero-fee model.

Prediction market volume surged more than 300% in 2025 to $63.5B. Robinhood reported prediction market revenue up 320% in Q1 2026. 

This sector is genuinely growing. Which is exactly why every major player is moving at the same time.

➝ Here's the competitive map right now:

Kalshi is regulated, U.S.-accessible, and just launched perpetual futures under the name Timeless. 

Polymarket is seeking CFTC approval to reopen to U.S. traders and launching 10x leveraged contracts on $BTC, Nvidia, and gold. 

Hyperliquid is restricted to non-U.S. users, has no regulatory constraints, and is moving faster than either of them.

The irony is structural. Kalshi bet that partnering with Hyperliquid would give it an onchain distribution layer it couldn't build alone. 

But in doing so it accelerated the arrival of the one competitor with the infrastructure to make Polymarket irrelevant in Asia the exact market both Kalshi and Polymarket haven't been able to crack.

HIP-4 is still testnet only. No mainnet date. Oracle infrastructure unresolved. Governance model unclear.

But the blueprint is written. And Kalshi wrote it.
